insight: drift-watch readers: Claude Code JSONL format has types: user, assistant, progress, file-history-snapshot, queue-operation. Only user/assistant contain messages. Model is in assistant message.model. Tool uses are content blocks with type=tool_use in assistant messages. Tool results are content blocks with type=tool_result in user messages.

drift-watch readers: Codex CLI JSONL uses top-level {timestamp, type, payload} structure. Types: session_meta (metadata), response_item (messages+tool calls), event_msg, turn_context. Role mapping: developer=user, user=assistant. Function calls use call_id to link function_call and function_call_output records.

drift-watch readers: Gemini CLI uses single JSON files (not JSONL) per session. Structure: {sessionId, projectHash, startTime, messages[]}. Message types: user, gemini, error. Content can be string or [{text}] array. Tool calls are embedded in gemini messages as toolCalls array with nested functionResponse results.
drift-watch project needs @types/node devDependency for Node.js built-in module types (node:fs, node:path, etc.) and import.meta.dirname. Add it early when starting Node.js TypeScript projects.

In drift-watch, SCHEMA_SQL must be inlined as a template literal string in migrations.ts rather than read from schema.sql via fs.readFileSync, because tsup bundles the code and __dirname points to dist/ at runtime where .sql files are not copied.

When building dynamic SQL updates with Object.entries(), always validate keys against a static allowlist (Set) before interpolation, even if TypeScript types constrain the keys at compile time. Runtime JS callers can bypass type constraints.

Use execFileSync instead of execSync when running CLI tools in Node.js. execFileSync bypasses the shell, preventing command injection. execSync with string interpolation is a security risk even with hardcoded callers.
MySQL FLOAT type returns imprecise values (e.g., 0.949999988079071 instead of 0.95). In vitest tests, use toBeCloseTo() instead of toBe() when asserting float column values from Dolt/MySQL.

When mocking child_process.spawn in vitest, use EventEmitter pattern: create a MockChildProcess extending EventEmitter with stdin (write/end mocks), stdout/stderr (EventEmitters), kill mock. Use process.nextTick or setTimeout to emit events asynchronously. For retry tests, use mockReturnValueOnce chaining to return different mock processes per invocation.

When spawning CLI processes that return JSON (like claude --print), always: (1) capture stderr alongside stdout for better error diagnostics, (2) truncate raw output in error messages to prevent log bloat, (3) use a settled boolean flag to prevent double-resolution from concurrent events (close + error can both fire).

In drift-watch analysis module: ClaudeRunner (src/analysis/runner.ts) handles CLI invocation with retry-once on JSON parse/zod failures. analyze() (src/analysis/index.ts) orchestrates runner across conversations. Tests mock at child_process level for runner and at module level for analyze. Runner returns AnalysisResponse ({findings}), analyze extracts and flattens to Finding[].

In drift-watch analysis module: ClaudeRunner (src/analysis/runner.ts) handles CLI invocation with retry-once on JSON parse/zod failures. analyze() (src/analysis/index.ts) orchestrates runner across conversations. Tests mock at child_process level for runner and at module level for analyze. Runner returns AnalysisResponse ({findings}), analyze extracts and flattens to Finding[]. Schema uses z.int() for severity (not z.number()). System prompt in prompt.ts uses CATEGORIES constant for DRY category listing.

When adding columns to existing Dolt/MySQL tables in migrations, always make ALTER TABLE idempotent by wrapping in try/catch and ignoring 'already exists' errors. A partial migration failure can leave schema_version unset while columns are already added, causing re-run failures.

In scan pipelines that store results to Dolt, always call doltCommit even when there are zero findings. Otherwise cursor state (stored in scan records) is lost on Dolt server restart since uncommitted changes are discarded.

For cursor-based incremental scanning, use scan start time (not completion time) as the cursor. Using wall-clock 'now' at completion creates a gap where files modified during the scan can be skipped in subsequent scans.

When adding new columns to an existing table in drift-watch, the integration tests (repository.test.ts) manually apply migrations. Any schema change requires updating the test setup to apply the new migration AND updating test data to include the new fields, otherwise tests cascade-fail from the first insertFinding call.

In drift-watch, mysql2 Connection.execute() requires params typed as (string|number)[] not unknown[]. Using unknown[] causes TS2769 overload mismatch. Always type SQL params as (string | number)[] when building parameterized queries.

In drift-watch, display modules (src/display/) should be wired into the command that uses them. Creating a module without importing it anywhere is dead code. The review phase caught formatSummary as unused — always verify new modules are actually called from the command flow.

When adding a generic method to an existing class (like runWithSchema on ClaudeRunner), refactor the private implementation to be generic and have the existing public method call it. This preserves backward compatibility while enabling new use cases. Pattern: public run() -> execGeneric(AnalysisResponseSchema), public runWithSchema<T>(schema) -> execGeneric(schema).

In drift-watch, new commands should follow the established pattern from report.ts: DoltServer(dataDir) -> connect() -> applyMigrations(conn) -> try { ... } finally { conn.end() }. Args parsing defaults should live in the main function via ?? operator, not in parseSuggestArgs. This was caught in review.

When TypeScript functions accept user-provided string keys for typed objects (e.g., config[key]), use 'string' parameter type with 'as keyof T' cast internally rather than 'keyof T' parameter type. The stricter type breaks when CLI passes process.argv strings. DTS generation (tsup) catches this at build time even if tsc --noEmit passes.

Cron expressions from user input are a command injection vector. Always validate format (5 space-separated fields, each matching /^[\d*,/-]+$/) before writing to crontab. Newline injection can add arbitrary cron jobs. This applies to any crontab-manipulating CLI tool.

In drift-watch migrations, use MySQL error codes (ER_DUP_FIELDNAME, ER_TABLE_EXISTS_ERROR) for idempotency checks in ALTER TABLE statements, NOT string matching on error messages. MySQL says 'Duplicate column name', not 'already exists'. String matching is locale-dependent and fragile. Supersedes L513dc0c6.

In drift-watch commands, applyMigrations(conn) must be INSIDE the try/finally block, not before it. If migrations throw (schema conflict, network), the connection leaks because conn.end() is only in the finally block. Pattern: const conn = await server.connect(); try { await applyMigrations(conn); ... } finally { await conn.end(); }

In Node.js CLI tools writing cron entries, always quote paths and binary names in the crontab entry string. Cron runs in a minimal shell environment - unquoted paths with spaces or shell metacharacters cause silent failures or injection. Use: '"binaryPath" scan >> "logsDir/file.log"' not bare interpolation.

When CLI arg parsers use args[++i] to read flag values, always check args.length bounds first. If a flag like --since is the last argument, args[++i] returns undefined, which flows into downstream code as the string 'undefined'. Guard with: if (i + 1 >= args.length) throw new Error('--flag requires a value').

When consolidating feedback from multiple code reviewers, deduplicate first — 4 reviews of drift-watch found 25 unique issues from ~50 total findings. Group fixes by file/concern for efficient parallel implementation. P1 bugs first, then data integrity, then reliability, then correctness.

When a CLI tool manages server processes via PID files, always verify the PID belongs to the expected process type before sending SIGTERM. Use 'ps -p PID -o comm=' to check the command name. A stale PID file can point to an unrelated process that reused the PID after a crash. In drift-watch, DoltServer.stop() now checks the process is actually 'dolt' before killing.

In batch processing pipelines (like drift-watch scan), use partial failure resilience: wrap each batch item in try/catch, accumulate errors, and only throw if ALL items fail. If some succeed, warn about failures but return partial results. This prevents one bad project group from losing findings from all other groups.

When spawning child processes with stdin piping (e.g., ClaudeRunner), attach a no-op handler to proc.stdin.on('error') to suppress EPIPE errors. The child process may exit before reading all input, causing an unhandled EPIPE that crashes the parent. This is especially important in timeout/retry scenarios where the process is killed mid-stream.

For retry logic with external processes (like LLM calls), use a deadline-based timeout budget instead of per-attempt timeouts. Pass a deadline timestamp (Date.now() + totalBudget) and check remaining time before each attempt. This prevents retries from extending total wall time beyond what the caller expects. Pattern: effectiveDeadline = deadline ?? Date.now() + budget; if (Date.now() >= effectiveDeadline) reject('budget exhausted').

In Node.js, never use '~' as a fallback for process.env.HOME. The tilde is not expanded by Node's fs module — use os.homedir() instead. process.env.HOME ?? os.homedir() is the correct pattern for resolving the user's home directory cross-platform.

When serializing nested objects for storage or display (like Gemini tool call results), use JSON.stringify() not String(). String() on an object produces '[object Object]', losing all data. This was caught in drift-watch's Gemini reader where tool_context was storing useless strings instead of the actual function response.

In drift-watch, new commands should follow this pattern: const conn = await server.connect(); try { await applyMigrations(conn); /* work */ } finally { await conn.end(); }. applyMigrations MUST be inside try/finally to prevent connection leaks on migration errors. Args parsing defaults should live in the main function via ?? operator.

When adding a 'runs' table to track command invocations (like suggest_runs mirroring scans), always wrap the generation/processing loop in try/catch and update the run record to 'failed' status in the catch block before re-throwing. Without this, orphaned 'running' records accumulate and confuse history queries.

In drift-watch, the repository.test.ts beforeAll must apply ALL migration versions in order (V1 through current). Skipping a migration version (e.g., V6 indexes) causes test schema to diverge from production, leading to confusing failures when queries depend on those migrations.

When adding a foreign key column in drift-watch migrations, always include the FOREIGN KEY constraint in the same migration SQL. The existing error handling in applyMigrations already covers ER_DUP_KEYNAME for idempotency. Pattern: ALTER TABLE x ADD COLUMN fk_col; CREATE INDEX; ALTER TABLE x ADD CONSTRAINT fk_name FOREIGN KEY (fk_col) REFERENCES parent(id).
